title: Sweet almond and rose geranium soap
subtitle: A conditioning cold-process bar with sweet almond oil and rose geranium essential oil
excerpt: A cold-process bar soap with sweet almond oil replacing part of the olive fraction for a lighter, faster-lathering bar. Scented with rose geranium essential oil. The blend is 40% olive, 30% coconut, 20% sweet almond, 10% castor. Allow 4-6 weeks to harden before use.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997): carrier oil profiles and formulation balance. SoapCalc (accessed May 2026): sweet almond oil SAP value 0.136. USDA ACS oil database: sweet almond linoleic and oleic acid profile.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 1 kg oil weight cleanly. The higher linoleic acid content from sweet almond reduces the shelf life versus an all-olive recipe; keep batches moderate.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cured bar keeps 18 months. Sweet almond oil is higher in linoleic acid than olive, which shortens the shelf life compared with pure olive bars.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 18 months from cure date on an open rack in a cool, dark place. Contains tree nut oil (sweet almond); label clearly if gifting. Discard on DOS (orange spots on the cut face) or a rancid smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The reaction between oil and sodium hydroxide that produces soap and glycerin. Sweet almond oil has a saponification value of 0.136, close to olive, which keeps the lye calculation straightforward.
The point at which the soap batter thickens enough to hold a surface trail. Sweet almond oil traces at a similar speed to olive, slightly faster than a bastille-style blend.
The percentage of oil left unreacted in the finished bar. At 5%, the sweet almond superfat fraction is high in oleic and linoleic acids, which condition the skin during washing.

content.text: Sweet almond oil in cold-process soap behaves similarly to olive oil but traces a little faster, produces a slightly lighter lather, and has a shorter shelf life because of its higher linoleic acid content. In this formula it replaces part of the olive fraction, giving a bar that is milder than a coconut-heavy recipe and softer than a pure bastille, with a clear floral scent from rose geranium essential oil.

Rose geranium (Pelargonium roseum) is a good essential oil for cold-process soap because it has reasonable tenacity in the alkaline environment; many citrus and floral oils fade badly, but geranium holds its character through cure. It has a soft, rosy-green scent without the sweetness of a true rose absolute.
Wear protective gloves and goggles throughout the lye work. Add sodium hydroxide to water, not the reverse.

items.prepNote: 20%. Cold-pressed cosmetic-grade. Contains tree nuts; declare on the finished bar if gifting or selling.
3% of oil weight. Add at light trace. Pelargonium roseum; good tenacity in alkaline soap versus many other florals.

content.text: Gloves and goggles on. Add the sodium hydroxide to the distilled water slowly and stir until dissolved. Allow to cool to 38-42°C.
Melt the coconut oil if solid. Combine all oils and allow to reach 38-42°C. Pour the lye solution into the oils in a slow stream. Stick-blend in 5-second bursts to
moves at a comparable pace to a standard olive-coconut recipe. At light trace, add the rose geranium essential oil and blend for 10 seconds. Pour into the mould immediately.
Insulate and leave 24-48 hours. Wear gloves to unmould and cut. Cure on an open rack for 4-6 weeks. The
fraction from the sweet almond oil gives a softer, more emollient feel than an all-olive bar.

Keep bars on an open rack in a cool, dark place. The higher linoleic acid content from the sweet almond oil means the shelf life is 18 months rather than the 2 years of a pure olive bar. Check for DOS (orange spots) and a paint-like rancidity smell at 12 months. Discard any bar showing either sign. Label the bar with the tree nut content if gifting.
